caseInventory: The chess suite is now 4 scenarios (down from 60: commit c188f4a removed the cloneChessScenario drill duplication flagged as prior finding #12), all in CHESS_BASE_SCENARIOS at lib/benchmark/gameiq/chess.ts:11-92: 2 mate-in-one (back-rank Re8#, queen Qg7#) and 2 legal-tactic (Nxd4 wins loose queen, a8=Q promotion). They feed the single pack "gameiq-v0.1-chess" (packs.ts:22-28, certificationTier "first-class") which is runtime-reachable: suite-options.ts lists all GameIQ packs, CertifiedRunPanel.tsx:712 selects them, runCertifiedGameIq (certified-runner.ts:85-115) executes each scenario as one temperature-0 structured-output model call, and runGameIqScenarios (runner.ts:122-217) scores them into the certified leaderboard (pass threshold 70 via statusFromScore, types.ts:173-175). I verified all four positions with the repo's own engine: Re8# is the unique mate among 20 legal moves, Qg7# the unique mate among 28, Nxd4 the unique queen capture among 7, and a8=Q legal (a8=R is also winning but the prompt explicitly constrains to queen, so exact-match is defensible). Tactics are correct, expected actions are the unique answers to the questions as asked, and validation (validateChessMateInOne / validateChessLegalTactic, validation.ts:317-348) replays them behaviorally. So there are 4 scenarios but, because of prompt-side leakage, only ~2 genuinely discriminating chess decisions (the two mates); the two legal-tactic scenarios are answerable by text pattern-matching without move evaluation.

findings.summary: The model-facing JSON shape example for chess is literally the correct answer to the knight-wins-queen scenario: gameIqActionShapeExample returns '{"action":{"from":"e2","to":"d4","promotion":null}}' and the scenario's expected action is from e2 to d4.
findings.evidence: lib/benchmark/gameiq/certified-runner.ts:329 (shape example e2->d4) is appended to every chess prompt by gameIqScenarioPrompt (certified-runner.ts:257-263); lib/benchmark/gameiq/chess.ts:64-66 expects {from:"e2",to:"d4"}. Traced end-to-end: an echo of the example passes isStructuredGameIqAction (validation.ts:108-112), validateChessAction (Ne2xd4 is legal), and actionsEqual's promotion compare `(left.promotion ?? null) === (right.promotion ?? null)` (validation.ts:380-385) — scored structured+legal+correct. A zero-chess-ability baseline that copies the example gets 1 of 4 scenarios (25% of outcomeScore/moveQuality) free, violating the checklist rule that a naive baseline must fail every meaningful assertion. Same defect pattern exists for other games' examples (fireworks example '{"action":"play",...,"cardIndex":0}' equals the expected action of all 20 basic + 30 memory fireworks scenarios).
findings.proposedImprovement: Change the chess shape example to squares that are illegal in every pack position (e.g. '{"action":{"from":"b1","to":"c3","promotion":null}}' — b1 is empty in all 4 FENs), or better, generate the example generically ('{"action":{"from":"<square>","to":"<square>","promotion":"queen"|null}}'). Add a guard test in scripts/test-gameiq-scenarios.mts asserting that for every pack, gameIqActionShapeExample's parsed action does not satisfy actionMatchesExpected for any scenario — this would also catch the fireworks/connect-four instances of the same leak.
verifierCorrections: The finding's `file` field is wrong: lib/benchmark/certified/certified-runner.ts does not exist. The correct path is lib/benchmark/gameiq/certified-runner.ts (line 329 is correct there; the evidence text already uses the right path). Minor precision note: outcomeScore/moveQuality are averaged over category groups (distinctGroupKey), not raw scenarios — for chess this still yields exactly 0.25 (groups: mate-in-one 0/2, legal-tactic 1/2), so the 25% figure stands but by group math rather than 1-of-4 scenario counting.

The finding's `file` field is wrong: lib/benchmark/certified/certified-runner.ts does not exist. The correct path is lib/benchmark/gameiq/certified-runner.ts (line 329 is accurate there; the evidence field already cites the right path). Also, the fireworks instance is worse than described: because runner.ts groups scoring by identical expectedActions, an example-echoing baseline scores ~100% (not just per-scenario credit) of outcomeScore/moveQuality on the gameiq-fireworks-basic-v1 and first-class gameiq-fireworks-memory-v1 packs, since every scenario in both packs expects {action:"play",cardIndex:0} and it is always legal. Connect-four is also affected concretely: the example {"column":3} equals the expected action of at least 3 scenarios (connect-four.ts lines 60, 84, 108-110). Severity "high" is justified.

The finding's `file` field path is wrong: lib/benchmark/certified/certified-runner.ts does not exist. The correct file is lib/benchmark/gameiq/certified-runner.ts (line 329 is correct in that file, and the evidence field already cites the right path). Additionally, the fireworks instance of the same defect is more severe than the chess one: it is not a 25% partial leak but a 100%-of-pack leak — every scenario in the 20-scenario basic pack and the 30-scenario memory-stress pack has expected action {action:"play",cardIndex:0}, identical (under fireworksActionsEqual, which ignores the null clue fields) to the prompt's shape example, so an example-echoing baseline fully passes those packs.

findings.summary: Scenario prompts and titles (both sent to the model) spell out the tactic and the moving piece, reducing the two legal-tactic scenarios to FEN square-reading and giving strong hints on the mates.
findings.evidence: gameIqScenarioPrompt (certified-runner.ts:258) includes scenario.title. chess.ts:59-60 prompt: "Return the legal knight move that wins black's queen" — the FEN (chess.ts:62) has exactly one knight (e2) and one black queen (d4), so the instruction uniquely determines the answer with zero move evaluation (engine confirms only 7 legal moves, 1 queen capture). chess.ts:79-80: "Return the legal promotion move that makes a queen" — one pawn (a7), answer fully determined; yet it is labeled difficulty "medium" (chess.ts:77). Titles leak tactic class: "back-rank mate in one" (chess.ts:15) tells the model the mating square is on rank 8; "queen mate in one" (chess.ts:35) plus prompt "legal queen move" names the piece. Net effect: a model with no chess evaluation scores outcome 0.5, legal 1.0, structured 1.0 = 65.5/100, and needs only ONE real mate-in-one solve to certify at 82.75 (>= 70 pass, types.ts:173-175).
findings.proposedImprovement: Use one neutral prompt for all chess scenarios ("White to move. Return the single best move as JSON.") and neutral model-facing titles ("Chess position 3 of 4") — keep the descriptive titles for the UI only by dropping scenario.title from gameIqScenarioPrompt or adding a separate modelTitle field. Category labels (mate-in-one/legal-tactic) should stay internal for the verifier. Re-grade difficulty labels once hints are removed (the promotion scenario is 'easy' at best).
verifierCorrections: Three minor precision notes: (a) the evidence's '65.5' shorthand omits that moveQuality=0.5 (weight 0.32) is included in the total — the arithmetic is correct but the metric enumeration is incomplete; (b) 'types.ts:173-175' refers to lib/benchmark/gameiq/types.ts (statusFromScore), not lib/benchmark/types.ts; the weight constants live in lib/benchmark/scoring/gameiq.ts; (c) the 65.5 baseline requires the model to still produce legal (non-mating) moves on the two mate scenarios, i.e. FEN parsing + legal-move generation with zero move evaluation — consistent with the finding's 'FEN square-reading' framing but worth stating explicitly. Severity 'medium' is appropriate.

Minor precision notes only: (a) the 65.5 baseline additionally assumes the model emits legal, structured moves on the two mate scenarios (basic rule competence, though still zero evaluation); (b) the 65.5/82.75 certification math applies to chess-pack-only certified runs — runCertifiedGameIq flatMaps all selected packs into one scored attempt, so multi-pack runs dilute the effect; (c) the back-rank title hint strongly narrows but does not literally uniquely determine Re8# (the model still needs the back-rank-mate concept the title names), unlike the two legal-tactic scenarios which are fully determined.

findings.summary: A 'first-class' certified pack labeled 'Chess Tactics' contains only 4 micro-positions (~2 real decisions after prompt leaks) — all white-to-move, all <=8 pieces, no distractor pieces, no defensive category, no wrong-capture traps — so the score has ~2 bits of chess signal and certification is passable while failing half the pack.
findings.evidence: chess.ts:11-92: 4 scenarios total; categories used are only mate-in-one and legal-tactic (block-win/avoid-losing-move exist in the GameIqScenarioCategory enum, types.ts:28-37, but no chess scenario uses them). Every position is a sparse constructed endgame (max 8 pieces incl. kings); engine enumeration shows decision spaces of 20/28/7/9 legal moves with zero tempting-but-wrong alternatives (no other checks in the back-rank position, no other captures anywhere). All are White to move; no castling, en passant, underpromotion, pins, or defended-piece traps. Score math: outcomeScore/moveQuality each take values in {0,.25,.5,.75,1}; 3/4 correct + all legal = 82.75 passes; even the 2 prompt-leaked tactics alone + legal moves = 65.5. Compare the sibling connect-four pack: 40 scenarios across 4 categories (test-gameiq-scenarios.mts:108-120).
findings.proposedImprovement: Grow the pack to 20+ genuinely distinct positions across at least 4 categories, each verified by the existing behavioral validators: (a) 6-8 more mate-in-one with distractor checks that don't mate (e.g. smothered mate Nf7# where Qh5+ exists; two-rook positions where only one rook mate works); (b) avoid-losing-move: a loose-looking queen that is actually defended (e.g. add a black pawn on c5 defending d4-style bait) where the expected action is NOT the capture — extend validateChessLegalTactic with a one-ply safety replay for this category; (c) block-win/defense: black threatens back-rank mate, expected move is the only defense (Luft or rook trade); (d) black-to-move scenarios; (e) underpromotion-required (a8=Q stalemates or loses, a8=N forks/mates) and en-passant-as-only-win. All are cheap to author since validateChessMateInOne already replays mates through the engine, and the distinct-tuple guard test (test-gameiq-scenarios.mts:64-85) already enforces distinctness.
verifierCorrections: 1) Replace "certification is passable while failing half the pack" with "certification is passable while failing a quarter of the pack": with 2/4 correct the maximum score is 65.5, which is below the 70 pass threshold (statusFromScore, lib/benchmark/gameiq/types.ts:173-175) and fails; 3/4 correct = 82.75 passes. 2) "max 8 pieces incl. kings" is wrong: the back-rank position (6k1/5ppp/8/8/8/8/5PPP/4R1K1) has 9 pieces; correct statement is "max 9 pieces". 3) "zero tempting-but-wrong alternatives" holds for checks only in the back-rank position and for captures pack-wide; the queen-mate position does have 3 non-mating checks (Qh1+, Qh2+, Qg8+) a model could wrongly pick. All other evidence (move counts 20/28/7/9, categories, line references, connect-four comparison, validator names) verified exactly correct.

1) knownFromPriorReview should be true for the core weakness: docs/bench/review-2026-07-01.md issue 12 documented the 4-distinct-position / ~2-bits problem, and docs/superpowers/plans/2026-07-01-benchmark-phase-2-medium.md:148-279 explicitly deferred authoring >=24 distinct positions as a pending follow-up (chess distinct floor deliberately set to 4). New in this finding: prompt leaks, missing defensive categories, all-white-to-move, no distractor moves. 2) Summary's "passable while failing half the pack" is false by the finding's own numbers: 2/4 correct = 65.5 < 70 fails; correct claim is passable while failing a quarter (82.75, or 75.0 even with a fully illegal answer on that quarter). 3) Back-rank position has 9 pieces, not <=8. 4) Queen-mate position does have non-mating checks (Qh1+, Qh2+, Qg8+), so "zero tempting-but-wrong alternatives" is slightly overstated there. 5) Severity better rated medium: the headline gap is already a tracked, planned known issue; only the incremental observations are novel.

1. Summary error: "certification is passable while failing half the pack" is false by the finding's own math — failing half (2/4 correct) yields 65.5, which is below the 70 pass threshold (statusFromScore) and returns failed_model. The correct statement: certification is passable while failing a quarter of the pack (3/4 correct + legal = 82.75 >= 70), i.e. a model can pass while missing one of the only two non-leaked decisions. The evidence's "65.5" figure is a failing score, not a passing one.

2. "all <=8 pieces" is slightly wrong: the back-rank position (6k1/5ppp/8/8/8/8/5PPP/4R1K1) has 9 pieces. Also "zero tempting-but-wrong alternatives" is overstated for the queen-mate position, which does have 3 non-mating distractor checks (Qh2+, Qg8+, Qh1+); the finding's narrower claims (no other checks in the back-rank position, no other captures anywhere) are accurate.

3. knownFromPriorReview should be true in substance: docs/bench/review-2026-07-01.md (issue #12) already characterized the chess pack as "4 effective samples (~2 bits)" and its suggested fix included authoring genuinely distinct positions, and docs/superpowers/plans/2026-07-01-benchmark-phase-2-medium.md proposed a distinct-tuple floor of 24 for gameiq-v0.1-chess ("expect >=24 distinct probes, not 4"). The implemented fix instead removed the 56 clones and pinned the floor at 4, leaving the authoring expansion undone. The genuinely new contributions of this finding are the category/coverage analysis (no defensive scenarios, all white-to-move, prompt leakage, distractor-free move spaces verified by engine enumeration) and the pass-threshold math. Given the core issue was previously reviewed (and rated medium there) with expansion explicitly deferred as optional follow-up, severity "high" is arguable; "medium" is more defensible for what is a known, partially-triaged coverage gap in an opt-in benchmark feature.

4. Implementation note for the proposed fix: growing the pack requires updating pinned scenario counts in scripts/review-benchmark-corpus.mts (currently 4) and scripts/test-gameiq-scenarios.mts (expectedPackCounts/distinctFloor), and scripts/test-benchmark-case-manifests.mts is already stale (still expects 60). Also, the avoid-losing-move/block-win categories currently have no chess behavioral validator (validateScenarioCategory only handles chess mate-in-one and legal-tactic), so the proposed one-ply safety replay is new validator code, not a tweak to validateChessLegalTactic alone.

findings.summary: Prior finding #12 (60 scenarios / 4 distinct FENs) — verified fixed on main: the clone-drill generation was deleted in commit c188f4a, the pack is now the 4 base scenarios, and runner.ts adds distinct-group aggregation plus a distinct-tuple guard test.
findings.evidence: git show c188f4a removed cloneChessScenario/GENERATED_CHESS_MATES/GENERATED_CHESS_TACTICS; chess.ts:94 now exports CHESS_BASE_SCENARIOS directly; test-gameiq-scenarios.mts:47-85 pins count=4 and distinct (initialState,expectedActions) tuples.
findings.proposedImprovement: No action beyond the coverage expansion above — but note the fix traded fake breadth for honest tininess (4 samples), so the coverage-gap finding is the real successor issue.
verifierCorrections: Only a minor precision nit: runner.ts's distinctGroupKey aggregates on (gameId, expectedActions), not on the (initialState, expectedActions) tuple the test guards — the finding's generic phrasing "distinct-group aggregation" is accurate but conflates the two keys slightly.

findings.summary: The metric de-duplication key distinctGroupKey omits initialState, so any two scenarios whose expectedActions coincide are silently merged and down-weighted — a latent trap for chess authoring and a live mis-aggregation for sibling packs scored by the same runner.
findings.evidence: runner.ts:46-51 keys groups on {gameId, expectedActions} only (GameIqScenarioResult carries no initialState). For chess today all 4 keys are distinct (verified by enumeration), but two future chess puzzles sharing the same from/to squares (common: many mates are delivered on the same coordinates from different positions) would count as one sample. The defect is already live in packs sharing this runner: my group-key scan shows gameiq-fireworks-memory-v1 collapses 30 distinct states to 1 group, gameiq-fireworks-basic-v1 20->2, gameiq-fireworks-hard-v1 40->4, and gameiq-v0.1-connect-four 40->28 (9 merged groups of genuinely different boards that share an expected column+label). The phase-2 plan (docs/superpowers/plans/2026-07-01-benchmark-phase-2-medium.md:190-197) explicitly considered adding initialState and skipped it, claiming expectedActions was sufficient — it only checked battleship/codenames, not connect-four/fireworks.
findings.proposedImprovement: Add initialState to GameIqScenarioResult in evaluateScenario (runner.ts:104-119) and include stableStringify(initialState) in distinctGroupKey, exactly as the plan's 'stricter key' note describes. Extend test-gameiq-scoring.mts with a case where two scenarios share expectedActions but differ in initialState and assert they aggregate as two groups.
verifierCorrections: Minor: the plan-doc citation should be docs/superpowers/plans/2026-07-01-benchmark-phase-2-medium.md:184 (the "If a stricter key is wanted... but expectedActions alone is sufficient and minimal here" note), not lines 190-197 (which is the before/after metric code block). Also a strengthening note: because certified-runner.ts:88-89 flattens multiple selected packs into a single runGameIqScenarios call, the under-keyed group key additionally merges/reweights across packs sharing a gameId (e.g., the three fireworks packs), which the finding does not mention. For gameiq-fireworks-memory-v1 in isolation the collapse to 1 group is numerically neutral (grouped average equals plain average); the live distortion is in basic/hard/connect-four (unequal group sizes) and any multi-pack run.

Two refinements. (a) Numeric impact: within a single-pack run the fireworks merges are currently score-neutral because every merged group happens to be equal-sized (memory: one group of 30 = plain mean; basic: 10+10; hard: 4x10 — mean-of-means equals the overall mean), so "down-weighted" is literally accurate today only for connect-four (21 scenarios in 9 merged groups of 2-3 get weights like 3/40→1/28) and for multi-pack certified runs, where certified-runner.ts:89 flatMaps selected packs into one scoring call and produces unequal groups (combined fireworks: 90→6 groups sized 10,40,10,10,10,10). The fireworks single-pack collapses are still wrong semantics (the "distinct sample" count is bogus and any pack edit or subset run changes weights unpredictably), but the live mis-weighting examples should cite connect-four and multi-pack runs. (b) The plan-doc reference is at approximately lines 168-184 (the "stricter key" note is line 184), not 190-197. Severity medium is appropriate.

findings.summary: The accepted promotion vocabulary ("queen"/"rook"/"bishop"/"knight") is never stated in the model-visible prompt text — the shape example only shows promotion:null — so on providers that don't enforce the JSON schema, a model answering UCI-style "q" or "Q" is scored illegal+incorrect on a chess-correct move.
findings.evidence: gameIqActionShapeExample (certified-runner.ts:329) shows only '"promotion":null'; the enum ["queen","rook","bishop","knight",null] lives solely in the structured-output schema (certified-runner.ts:29-36), whose enforcement is provider-dependent (model-call.ts passes it through; text-mode providers get no constraint). Validation is exact-string: isStructuredGameIqAction accepts any string (validation.ts:111), then isLegalMove requires m.promotion === move.promotion against engine PieceType "queen" (lib/games/chess/types.ts:9, engine.ts:664-672), and actionsEqual compares raw strings (validation.ts:380-385). So {"from":"a7","to":"a8","promotion":"q"} fails as 'Illegal chess move' — measuring output-format luck, not chess.
findings.proposedImprovement: Two-line fix in the harness: (1) state the allowed values in the chess shape example ('"promotion": one of "queen"|"rook"|"bishop"|"knight" or null'); (2) normalize common synonyms before validation (q/Q/queen, n/N/knight, etc.) in a small mapper applied in the chess branch of validateGameIqAction/actionsEqual. Add a scoring test asserting promotion "q" is accepted as "queen".
verifierCorrections: The finding's file path is wrong: it cites lib/benchmark/certified/certified-runner.ts, but the file is lib/benchmark/gameiq/certified-runner.ts (all cited line numbers — 261, 329, 29-36 — match the gameiq file exactly). Also, the evidence understates the custom-provider case: lib/providers/structured-output.ts omits response_format entirely for any provider other than openai/openrouter, so custom endpoints receive no schema at all, and Anthropic's tool input_schema enum is advisory rather than strictly enforced.

File path is wrong: the cited file is lib/benchmark/gameiq/certified-runner.ts, not lib/benchmark/certified/certified-runner.ts (which does not exist); the cited line numbers (261 prompt line, 329 shape example, ~29-36 enum) are correct in the actual file. Impact is slightly understated: runner.ts:111 computes structured = structuredShape && validation.ok, so a chess-correct "q" answer is additionally counted as a structured-output failure (drags structuredReliability), not just illegal+incorrect. Provider-surface precision: custom OpenAI-compatible endpoints receive no schema at all (structured-output.ts only emits response_format for "openai"/"openrouter"); Anthropic receives the schema as a forced tool input_schema, so the enum IS model-visible there (via tool definition) though not hard-enforced; OpenAI (strict:true) and Google (enum preserved in responseSchema) do enforce. Minor mitigating detail worth noting: the scenario prompt text says "makes a queen" in English, which nudges toward the right piece but still not the accepted JSON string values.

findings.summary: validateChessLegalTactic only asserts the expected action is 'any capture or any promotion', far weaker than the scenarios' claims ('wins black's queen', 'makes a queen') — it would accept a scenario whose expected capture loses material to a recapture.
findings.evidence: validation.ts:331-348: the category validator checks `isCapture || isPromotion` and nothing else — not the captured piece's type/value, not whether the capturing piece is safe afterwards, not the promotion piece. The stronger check (captured piece is a black queen) exists only as a one-off assertion for one scenario in scripts/test-gameiq-scenarios.mts:320-339, not in the pack-integrity validator that guards all future authoring. Current 4 scenarios happen to be sound (engine-verified), so this is a guard gap, not a live mis-score.
findings.proposedImprovement: Strengthen validateChessLegalTactic to behaviorally verify the claim: for capture tactics, require the captured piece to be worth >= the tag/note's claimed material (or simply >= a rook) AND replay one ply to assert no legal opponent reply recaptures on the target square with a lower-valued piece winning the exchange; for promotion tactics, assert the expected promotion piece matches the scenario prompt. This keeps the verifier behavioral (engine-replayed) per docs/bench/case-authoring.md and protects the coverage expansion proposed above.
verifierCorrections: Minor precision only: (1) legality of expected actions IS separately engine-verified — validateGameIqScenario runs validateChessAction/isLegalMove on every expected action before the category check — so the weakness is confined to tactical strength, not legality; the finding's "only asserts any capture or any promotion" refers correctly to the category validator alone. (2) The isCapture heuristic (piece on target square) would also miss en-passant captures, an additional un-cited gap. Severity and proposed fix are appropriate as stated.

The proposed ">= a rook" material threshold is arbitrary and would reject legitimate future scenarios that win a hanging minor piece; prefer a one-ply engine-replayed static-exchange sanity check (expected move nets positive material against best immediate recapture on the destination square), which subsumes the threshold. Also "assert the expected promotion piece matches the scenario prompt" is not mechanically checkable against free-text prompts; the practical guard is requiring promotion === "queen" unless a tag opts into under-promotion. Note the scoring path already exact-matches the promotion field (actionsEqual, validation.ts:380-386), so the gap is confined to authoring-time pack integrity, consistent with the finding's framing.
